source: Heller House — SpaceX CFO Bret Johnsen interview — financial mechanics, Starship timeline, orbital compute, AI business
summary: Direct-to-cell (direct-to-device) at 5G quality within two years is a new high-margin revenue stream and a structural threat to terrestrial cellular carriers.
summary_extended: Baker (via SpaceX CFO Bret Johnsen) argues that SpaceX's Starlink direct-to-device service will achieve 5G-quality connectivity within two years, enabling seamless global coverage from any standard handset without dead zones. Johnsen frames this as a product that terrestrial carriers structurally cannot replicate, as it requires a low-Earth orbit satellite constellation at scale — a moat no ground-based competitor can match. The investment implication is that this represents a new high-margin revenue stream for SpaceX (private, not directly investable) while posing a structural threat to terrestrial cellular carriers whose rural and roaming revenue could be displaced. No specific financial figures were cited, but the CFO described the two-year timeline as a near-certain product roadmap item rather than speculative, lending high conviction to the near-term commercialization thesis.
detail: Johnsen states next-gen direct-to-device will be '5G quality' within two years, enabling global roaming with no dead zones from any standard handset. This is framed as a product nobody else can replicate terrestrially and one consumers would pay a premium for.
confidence_evidence: CFO gives explicit two-year timeline and describes it as a near-certain product roadmap item, not speculative.
quote_fragment: next generation of direct to cell will be 5G quality in the next two years

ticker_audit.reasoning: T-Mobile is a major US terrestrial carrier directly threatened by Starlink direct-to-device achieving 5G quality; it is also a current Starlink distribution partner, making it both a potential beneficiary and a structural risk — the thesis implies carriers like TMUS face disruption to roaming and rural revenue.
AT&T is a major US terrestrial carrier whose rural and roaming revenue streams are directly threatened by Starlink direct-to-device at 5G quality, making it a direct subject of the disruption thesis.
Verizon is a major US terrestrial carrier directly threatened by Starlink direct-to-device displacing rural and roaming revenue, making it a direct subject of the disruption thesis.
